region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; commonly served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Flatbread with cooked chicken; mixed grain and protein dish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a chapati entry but does not have a specific combined chapati-with-chicken entry; closest_parent used for the flatbread component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX covers chapati flatbread; no combined chapati-with-chicken composite code exists; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for chapati with chicken as a combined dish.

preparation_method: pan-cooked chicken curry or dry-spiced chicken

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving consists of approximately two chapati pieces (about 120 g total) accompanied by a portion of spiced chicken (about 160 g), totalling approximately 280 g per plate as typically served at Indian eateries in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Chapati (whole wheat) and Chicken curry composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are derived by combining ICMR-NIN data for whole wheat chapati (per 100 g cooked) and ICMR-NIN data for chicken curry (per 100 g), then computing a weighted composite at approximately 43% chapati and 57% chicken by weight to reflect a typical Singapore Indian eatery serving. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked whole wheat flatbread (NDB 18960) and roasted/stewed chicken (NDB 05064) for plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Chapati, whole wheat
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for whole wheat chapati (glucose reference = 100). The chicken component does not contribute glycemic carbohydrates. The composite GI is dominated by the chapati component; GI 52 is applied to the whole dish as a defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 51.8 g carbohydrate in 280 g serving) / 100 ≈ 27.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full serving (280 g) contributes approximately 868 mg sodium, representing about 38% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to one chapati with a moderate chicken serving to keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Whole wheat chapati has a low GI of 52, but the glycemic load per full serving is approximately 27, which is high; carbohydrate portion control is essential.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include whole wheat chapati with chicken as part of a balanced meal, but should limit to one chapati piece per sitting and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is relatively low at 1.5 g per 100 g; whole wheat flour provides dietary fibre (2.2 g per 100 g) which supports LDL cholesterol management.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chapati with chicken is a relatively heart-friendly choice when prepared with minimal oil; patients with cardiovascular disease should choose lean chicken and avoid ghee-based preparation to keep saturated fat intake low.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chapati with Chicken is a popular Indian dish served widely at Indian eateries and hawker stalls in Singapore. Chapati is an unleavened flatbread made from whole wheat flour (atta), water, and a pinch of salt, rolled thin and dry-roasted on a tawa (flat griddle) until lightly charred spots appear. It is soft, slightly chewy, and mildly nutty in flavour. The chicken component is typically a dry-spiced or semi-gravy preparation using boneless or bone-in chicken pieces cooked with onions, tomatoes, and a blend of Indian spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, and chilli. Together, the dish provides a balanced combination of complex carbohydrates from whole wheat and lean protein from chicken. Nutritionally, a 280 g serving delivers approximately 490 kcal, 35 g protein, 16 g fat, and 52 g carbohydrate. The whole wheat chapati contributes dietary fibre and B vitamins, while the chicken provides iron, zinc, and vitamin B12. The glycemic index of whole wheat chapati is approximately 52 (low GI), making this dish a more glycaemically favourable choice compared to white rice-based meals. Sodium content is moderate and depends heavily on preparation style. Clinically, this dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled, and is a reasonable option for cardiovascular health when prepared with minimal saturated fat.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chapati with chicken?
What is the GI of chapati?
Is chapati with chicken high in sodium?
Can I eat chapati with chicken if I have high blood pressure?
What nutrients does chapati with chicken provide?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN data for chapati and chicken curry components combined as a weighted composite, cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy. No single approved source entry exists for the exact combined dish 'chapati with chicken' as served in Singapore. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for whole wheat chapati and applied as an analogous estimate to the composite dish. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact restaurant preparation at Baba Eating House is unknown and the nutrient values reflect a typical composite estimate.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in whole wheat chapati and lean chicken preparations per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy — confirmed zero. Nutrient values represent a weighted composite of approximately 43% chapati and 57% chicken by serving weight. Serving size of 280 g is an estimate based on typical Singapore Indian eatery portions (2 chapati pieces ~120 g + chicken portion ~160 g). The GI of 52 is applied from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole wheat chapati; the chicken component contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Glycemic load per serving = (52 × 51.8 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 27.0. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record as the dish is protein-anchored in the Singapore context.

Iteration 1: Populated FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology with closest_parent match types; SNOMED CT set to none with gap note.
Iteration 1: Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-absence justification in notes.
Iteration 1: Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency — all language entries present verbatim in flat aliases.
Iteration 2: Verified GI category threshold: GI 52 ≤ 55 → gi_category = 'low' confirmed correct. Score confirmed 8/8.
